title: Adding New Strategies
text: File: R/strategy_.R First, create user-facing constructor function validates user inputs instantiates strategy object. convention, function named strategy_<method_name>(). must call internal new_strategy() function, passing class name validated arguments.
code: #' @rdname strategy #'  #' @section Custom Method: #' Describe the statistical theory behind the new method here. #'  #' @param trt_var Treatment variable name #' @param my_custom_arg Description of your custom argument #'  #' @return `custom_method` class object #' @export strategy_custom_method <- function(formula = NULL,                                    family = gaussian(link = "identity"),                                    trt_var = NULL,                                    my_custom_arg = 100) {      # 1. Validate inputs using built-in helpers   check_formula(formula, trt_var)   check_family(family)      if (my_custom_arg <= 0) {     stop("my_custom_arg must be greater than 0.")   }      # 2. Bundle arguments   args <- list(formula = formula,                family = family,                trt_var = get_treatment_name(formula, trt_var),                my_custom_arg = my_custom_arg)      # 3. Create object   do.call(new_strategy, c(strategy = "custom_method", args)) }

what: Step 3: Write the Core Calculation Function
title: Adding New Strategies
text: File: Create new file (e.g., R/custom_method.R) add existing calculation files. Write function actually performs maths. receive two arguments: strategy (object created Step 1) analysis_params (list containing pre-processed ipd, ald, treatment labels, etc.). Crucial Requirement: function MUST return list containing exactly two elements: means model. means$: Estimates comparator treatment group (can scalar, vector bootstrap/MCMC draws). means$C: Estimates reference treatment group (can scalar, vector bootstrap/MCMC draws). model: list underlying models diagnostics (e.g., weights, glm fit objects) want returned user.
code: #' Calculate Custom Method #' @keywords internal calc_custom_method <- function(strategy, analysis_params) {      # Extract data and parameters   ipd <- analysis_params$ipd   ald <- analysis_params$ald   formula <- strategy$formula   custom_val <- strategy$my_custom_arg      # ... [PERFORM YOUR STATISTICAL MODELLING HERE] ...   # e.g., fit a model, run bootstraps, calculate weights      # Construct the strict return object   list(     means = list(       A = estimated_means_for_A,        C = estimated_means_for_C     ),     model = list(       fit = my_fitted_model,       custom_val = custom_val     )   ) }

what: Step 4: Register the S3 Dispatch Method
title: Adding New Strategies
text: File: R/calc_IPD_stats.R plug core function outstandR’s top-level execution, must register S3 method calc_IPD_stats. outstandR handles relative treatment effect transformations (ATE) variance estimation automatically, simply pass core function IPD_stat_factory().
code: #' @rdname calc_IPD_stats #' @section Custom Method statistics: #' Description of how the custom method calculates statistics. #' @export calc_IPD_stats.custom_method <- IPD_stat_factory(calc_custom_method)

text: File: R/print-strategies.R Finally, create nice console printout user types name instantiated strategy. Extend generic print.strategy using NextMethod() print common fields, print specific parameters.
code: #' @export #' @method print custom_method print.custom_method <- function(x, ...) {   # Print the standard strategy info (formula, family)   NextMethod()       # Print custom subclass info   cat(pillar::style_subtle("  Parameters:"), "\n")   cat("    Custom Argument:     ", x$my_custom_arg, "\n")      invisible(x) }

Good things include: raw fitted model objects (e.g., glm stanfit), calculated weights, convergence status flags, effective sample sizes (ESS). allows users extract model final outstandR object check diagnostics .

previous_headings: Best Practices for Writing a Good Strategy
title: Adding New Strategies
text: Many population adjustment methods rely optimization iterative fitting (like GLMs MAIC). internal model fails converge, function shouldn’t crash entire R session—especially bootstrap loop. Wrap volatile model fitting tryCatch(). fit fails, return NA means pass warning user.
code: fit <- tryCatch({   glm(formula, data = ipd, family = family) }, warning = function(w) {   # Handle warnings (e.g., probabilities numerically 0 or 1)   glm(formula, data = ipd, family = family) }, error = function(e) {   NULL # Return NULL on hard failure })  if (is.null(fit)) return(list(means = list(A = NA, C = NA), model = list(converged = FALSE)))

title: Simulating Target Cohorts with Custom Copulas
text: G-computation MIM, outstandR simulates synthetic pseudo-population reflecting ALD trial using Gaussian copula. approach decouples dependence structure (correlation matrix Σ\Sigma) marginal distributions covariates. simulation follows three steps: 1. Draw samples multivariate normal: Zi∼MVNK(0,Σ)Z_i \sim MVN_K(0,\Sigma) 2. Apply Probability Integral Transform: Uik=Φ(Zik)U_{ik}=\Phi(Z_{ik}) 3. Map target scales using inverse CDFs: Xik=Fk−1(Uik;θk)X_{ik}=F_k^{-1}(U_{ik};\theta_k) First, let’s load package prepare mixed continuous/binary outcome data.
code: library(outstandR) data(AC_IPD_contY_mixedX)  data(BC_ALD_contY_mixedX)   # Define mixed outcome regression formula lin_form_mixedX <- as.formula("y ~ X1 + X2 + X3 + trt + trt:(X1 + X2 + X4)")

title: Simulating Target Cohorts with Custom Copulas
text: default, package assumes multivariate normal distribution. However, users can define specific marginal distributions using marginal_distns argument. outstandR automatically retrieves reported means SDs ALD parameterize via method moments. Supported marginal distributions include: * "gamma" (Gamma) * "binom" (Binomial) * "norm" (Normal)
code: custom_distns <- c(X1 = "gamma", X2 = "binom")  outstandR_custom <- outstandR(   ipd_trial = AC_IPD_contY_mixedX,   ald_trial = BC_ALD_contY_mixedX,   strategy = strategy_gcomp_ml(     formula = list(outcome_model = lin_form_mixedX),     family = gaussian(link = "identity"),     marginal_distns = custom_distns,     N = 1000),   seed = 12345 )  print(outstandR_custom) #> Object of class 'outstandR'  #> ITC algorithm: GCOMP_ML  #> Model: gaussian  #> Scale: mean_difference  #> Common treatment: C  #> Individual patient data study: A vs C  #> Aggregate level data study: B vs C  #> Confidence interval level: 0.95  #>  #> Contrasts: #>  #> # A tibble: 3 × 5 #>   Treatments Estimate Std.Error lower.0.95 upper.0.95 #>   <chr>         <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 AB           -0.293    0.0930     -0.891      0.305 #> 2 AC           -1.22     0.0619     -1.71      -0.732 #> 3 BC           -0.926    0.0311     -1.27      -0.581 #>  #> Absolute: #>  #> # A tibble: 3 × 5 #>   Treatments Estimate Std.Error lower.0.95 upper.0.95 #>   <chr>         <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 A            -0.507    0.0176     -0.767     -0.247 #> 2 B            -0.214    0.0804     -0.769      0.342 #> 3 C             0.713    0.0493      0.277      1.15

title: Simulating Target Cohorts with Custom Copulas
text: sensitivity analyses, method moments conversion isn’t natively supported, can manually override target parameters using marginal_params. instance, simulate older cohort (mean = 70, SD = 10.5) covariate X1 using Gamma distribution:
code: target_mean <- 70 target_sd <- 10.5  sens_params <- list(   X1 = list(shape = (target_mean/target_sd)^2,             rate = target_mean/(target_sd^2)) )  outstandR_sens <- outstandR(   ipd_trial = AC_IPD_contY_mixedX,   ald_trial = BC_ALD_contY_mixedX,   strategy = strategy_gcomp_ml(     formula = list(outcome_model = lin_form_mixedX),     family = gaussian(link = "identity"),     marginal_distns = c(X1 = "gamma"),     marginal_params = sens_params,     N = 1000),   seed = 12345 )  print(outstandR_sens) #> Object of class 'outstandR'  #> ITC algorithm: GCOMP_ML  #> Model: gaussian  #> Scale: mean_difference  #> Common treatment: C  #> Individual patient data study: A vs C  #> Aggregate level data study: B vs C  #> Confidence interval level: 0.95  #>  #> Contrasts: #>  #> # A tibble: 3 × 5 #>   Treatments Estimate Std.Error lower.0.95 upper.0.95 #>   <chr>         <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 AB          -22.8   1073.         -87.1      41.4   #> 2 AC          -23.8   1073.         -88.0      40.4   #> 3 BC           -0.926    0.0311      -1.27     -0.581 #>  #> Absolute: #>  #> # A tibble: 3 × 5 #>   Treatments Estimate Std.Error lower.0.95 upper.0.95 #>   <chr>         <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 A              62.1      260.       30.5       93.8 #> 2 B              85.0      853.       27.7      142.  #> 3 C              85.9      853.       28.7      143.

title: Advanced Features: Diagnostics and Uncertainty Quantification
text: vignette demonstrates access model diagnostics, check weight distributions, perform robust variance estimation using objects returned outstandR. First, let’s load packages prepare data models use throughout examples.
code: library(outstandR) library(ggplot2)  data(AC_IPD_binY_contX) # A vs C IPD data(BC_ALD_binY_contX) # B vs C ALD  # Formulas lin_form_contX <- as.formula("y ~ PF_cont_1 + PF_cont_2 + trt + trt:EM_cont_1 + trt:EM_cont_2") bal_form_contX <- as.formula("~ EM_cont_1 + EM_cont_2")

title: Advanced Features: Diagnostics and Uncertainty Quantification
text: Standard errors population-adjusted analyses can sensitive model misspecification. outstandR supports robust (sandwich) standard errors MAIC ML-based G-computation. Variance approximated using delta method: Var̂(Δ̂)=∇g(θ̂)⊤⋅Vsand⋅∇g(θ̂)\hat{Var}(\hat{\Delta})=\nabla g(\hat{\theta})^\top\cdot V_{sand}\cdot\nabla g(\hat{\theta}) use , set var_method = "sandwich" outstandR() call:
code: stc_robust <- outstandR(   ipd_trial = AC_IPD_binY_contX,   ald_trial = BC_ALD_binY_contX,   strategy = strategy_gcomp_ml(     formula = list(outcome_model = lin_form_contX),     family = binomial()),   seed = 12345,   var_method = "sandwich" )  print(stc_robust) #> Object of class 'outstandR'  #> ITC algorithm: GCOMP_ML  #> Model: binomial  #> Scale: log_odds  #> Common treatment: C  #> Individual patient data study: A vs C  #> Aggregate level data study: B vs C  #> Confidence interval level: 0.95  #>  #> Contrasts: #>  #> # A tibble: 3 × 5 #>   Treatments Estimate Std.Error lower.0.95 upper.0.95 #>   <chr>         <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 AB            0.748     0.228     -0.187    1.68    #> 2 AC           -0.696     0.126     -1.39    -0.00128 #> 3 BC           -1.44      0.102     -2.07    -0.817   #>  #> Absolute: #>  #> # A tibble: 3 × 5 #>   Treatments Estimate Std.Error lower.0.95 upper.0.95 #>   <chr>         <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 A             0.293   0.00285      0.188      0.398 #> 2 B            -0.993   0.107       -1.63      -0.351 #> 3 C             0.451   0.00489      0.314      0.588

title: Advanced Features: Diagnostics and Uncertainty Quantification
text: MAIC, can extract calculated weights check extreme outliers view Effective Sample Size (ESS). First, run MAIC model: can inspect Effective Sample Size (ESS) assess suffered severe loss power due matching: Next, can plot histogram weights check extreme dominant weights:
code: outstandR_maic <- outstandR(   ipd_trial = AC_IPD_binY_contX,   ald_trial = BC_ALD_binY_contX,   strategy = strategy_maic(     formula = list(outcome_model = formula("y ~ trt"),                    balance_model = bal_form_contX),     family = binomial(link = "logit")),   seed = 12345 ) # Check Effective Sample Size print(outstandR_maic$model$ESS) #>      ESS  #> 136.8594 # Plot a histogram of weights ggplot(data.frame(weights = outstandR_maic$model$weights), aes(x = weights)) +   geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.1, color = "black", fill = "lightgray") +   theme_minimal() +   labs(title = "Distribution of MAIC Weights", x = "Weight", y = "Frequency")

title: Advanced Features: Diagnostics and Uncertainty Quantification
text: Bayesian G-computation MIM, underlying stanfit object retained output. First, let’s fit Bayesian G-computation strategy: can extract fit pass packages like bayesplot (installed) use rstanarm’s built-plotting assess MCMC convergence:  bayesplot available, standard convergence diagnostics summary() available:
code: outstandR_gcomp_bayes <- outstandR(   ipd_trial = AC_IPD_binY_contX,   ald_trial = BC_ALD_binY_contX,   strategy = strategy_gcomp_bayes(     formula = list(outcome_model = lin_form_contX),     family = binomial(link = "logit")),   seed = 12345,   iter = 250,   chains = 1,   refresh = 0 ) library(bayesplot) stan_obj <- outstandR_gcomp_bayes$model$fit mcmc_trace(stan_obj, pars = c("(Intercept)", "trtA")) summary(outstandR_gcomp_bayes$model$fit, pars = c("(Intercept)", "trtA")) #>  #> Model Info: #>  function:     stan_glm #>  family:       binomial [logit] #>  formula:      y ~ PF_cont_1 + PF_cont_2 + trt + trt:EM_cont_1 + trt:EM_cont_2 #>  algorithm:    sampling #>  sample:       125 (posterior sample size) #>  priors:       see help('prior_summary') #>  observations: 200 #>  predictors:   8 #>  #> Estimates: #>               mean   sd   10%   50%   90% #> (Intercept) -0.3    0.6 -0.9  -0.3   0.5  #> trtA        -3.0    0.7 -3.9  -3.1  -2.1  #>  #> MCMC diagnostics #>             mcse Rhat n_eff #> (Intercept) 0.0  1.0  145   #> trtA        0.1  1.0   38   #>  #> For each parameter, mcse is Monte Carlo standard error, n_eff is a crude measure of effective sample size, and Rhat is the potential scale reduction factor on split chains (at convergence Rhat=1).

title: Methodological Background
text: Indirect treatment comparisons (ITCs) vital head--head clinical trials absent. Unadjusted comparisons biased trial populations differ effect modifiers; thus, population adjustment required. outstandR provides unified framework methods.

title: Methodological Background
text: MAIC method--moments weighting approach designed match aggregate characteristics comparator trial. Weights wiw_i patient ii IPD trial derived via logistic regression formulation: wi=exp(β⊤Xi)w_i=\exp(\beta^\top X_i) parameters β\beta found minimizing convex objective function match target covariate means: minβ∈ℝp∑=1nAexp(β⊤(Xi−X¯B))\min_{\beta\\mathbb{R}^p}\sum_{=1}^{n_A}\exp(\beta^\top(X_i-\overline{X}_B))

title: Methodological Background
text: individual ii outcome YiY_i, treatment Ti∈{0,1}T_i \\{0,1\}, baseline covariates XiX_i, general outcome model : g(𝔼[Yi|Xi,Ti])=α+Xi⊤β0+(βtrt+Xi⊤β1)𝕀(Ti=1)g(\mathbb{E}[Y_i|X_i,T_i])=\alpha+X_i^\top\beta_0+(\beta_{trt}+X_i^\top\beta_1)\mathbb{}(T_i=1)

title: Methodological Background
text: G-computation standardizes outcomes across treatment regimens. estimate marginal mean outcome simulating pseudo-population size NN reflecting target ALD trial, predicting outcomes, averaging: 𝔼̂[YT|θ̂]=1N∑=1NYî(T,θ̂)\hat{\mathbb{E}}[Y^T|\hat{\theta}]=\frac{1}{N}\sum_{=1}^N\hat{Y_i}(T,\hat{\theta})

title: Methodological Background
text: Bayesian G-computation estimates full posterior distribution model parameters, offering robust uncertainty quantification. MM posterior samples θ(m)\theta^{(m)}, marginal mean computed : 𝔼̂[YT|θ(m)]=1N∑=1NŶ(T,θ(m))\hat{\mathbb{E}}[Y^T|\theta^{(m)}]=\frac{1}{N}\sum_{=1}^N\hat{Y}_i(T,\theta^{(m)})

title: Methodological Background
text: MIM conceptualizes unobserved potential outcomes missing data problem. Using MM posterior draws, MIM imputes counterfactuals utilizes modified Rubin’s rules calculate total variance subtracting within-imputation variance (U¯\overline{U}) -imputation variance (BB): Var(Q¯)=B−U¯Var(\overline{Q})=B-\overline{U}

title: Getting Started with outstandR: A Practical Demonstration
text: outstandR package provides unified framework Population-Adjusted Indirect Comparisons (PAIC). supports Matching-Adjusted Indirect Comparison (MAIC), G-computation via Maximum Likelihood Estimation (ML) Bayesian MCMC inference, Multiple Imputation Marginalization (MIM). vignette walks practical demonstration load data, specify strategies, run analyses, compare results across different outcome types: binary, continuous, count.

title: Getting Started with outstandR: A Practical Demonstration
text: load synthetic binary outcome data continuous covariates. regression-based adjustments (G-computation MIM), define formula relating covariates, treatments, outcome. weight-based adjustments (MAIC), specify balancing formula covariates match.
code: library(outstandR) data(AC_IPD_binY_contX) # A vs C IPD data(BC_ALD_binY_contX) # B vs C ALD # Outcome model formula (for G-computation and MIM) lin_form_bin <- as.formula("y ~ PF_cont_1 + PF_cont_2 + trt + trt:EM_cont_1 + trt:EM_cont_2")  # Balancing model formula (for MAIC) bal_form_bin <- as.formula("~ EM_cont_1 + EM_cont_2")

title: Getting Started with outstandR: A Practical Demonstration
text: can run four strategy classes. Bayesian methods, specify fewer iterations single chain speed demonstration.
code: # 1. Matching-Adjusted Indirect Comparison (MAIC) outstandR_maic_bin <- outstandR(   ipd_trial = AC_IPD_binY_contX,   ald_trial = BC_ALD_binY_contX,   strategy = strategy_maic(     formula = list(outcome_model = formula("y ~ trt"),                    balance_model = bal_form_bin),     family = binomial(link = "logit")),   seed = 12345 )  # 2. Maximum Likelihood G-computation (ML G-comp) outstandR_gcomp_ml_bin <- outstandR(   ipd_trial = AC_IPD_binY_contX,   ald_trial = BC_ALD_binY_contX,   strategy = strategy_gcomp_ml(     formula = list(outcome_model = lin_form_bin),     family = binomial(link = "logit")),   seed = 12345 )  # 3. Bayesian G-computation outstandR_gcomp_bayes_bin <- outstandR(   ipd_trial = AC_IPD_binY_contX,   ald_trial = BC_ALD_binY_contX,   strategy = strategy_gcomp_bayes(     formula = list(outcome_model = lin_form_bin),     family = binomial(link = "logit")),   seed = 12345,   iter = 250,      # Small iterations for speed   chains = 1,      # Single chain for demonstration   refresh = 0 )  # 4. Multiple Imputation Marginalization (MIM) outstandR_mim_bin <- outstandR(   ipd_trial = AC_IPD_binY_contX,   ald_trial = BC_ALD_binY_contX,   strategy = strategy_mim(     formula = list(outcome_model = lin_form_bin),     family = binomial(link = "logit")),   seed = 12345,   iter = 250,   chains = 1,   refresh = 0 )

text: default, package reports effects link function’s scale. can customize scale interest using scale argument. instance, can specify Risk Difference instead Log-Odds binary outcome analysis:
code: outstandR_maic_rd <- outstandR(   ipd_trial = AC_IPD_binY_contX,   ald_trial = BC_ALD_binY_contX,   strategy = strategy_maic(     formula = list(outcome_model = formula("y ~ trt"),                    balance_model = bal_form_bin),     family = binomial(link = "logit")),   scale = "risk_difference",   seed = 12345 )  print(outstandR_maic_rd) #> Object of class 'outstandR'  #> ITC algorithm: MAIC  #> Model: binomial  #> Scale: risk_difference  #> Common treatment: C  #> Individual patient data study: A vs C  #> Aggregate level data study: B vs C  #> Confidence interval level: 0.95  #>  #> Contrasts: #>  #> # A tibble: 3 × 5 #>   Treatments Estimate Std.Error lower.0.95 upper.0.95 #>   <chr>         <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 AB            0.165   0.443       -1.14      1.47   #> 2 AC           -0.177   0.00704     -0.341    -0.0121 #> 3 BC           -0.341   0.436       -1.63      0.952  #>  #> Absolute: #>  #> # A tibble: 3 × 5 #>   Treatments Estimate Std.Error lower.0.95 upper.0.95 #>   <chr>         <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 A            0.241    0.00208      0.151      0.330 #> 2 B            0.0760   0.441       -1.23       1.38  #> 3 C            0.417    0.00534      0.274      0.561

text: outstandR R package population-adjusted indirect treatment comparison (ITC). implements various methods adjust cross-trial differences effect modifiers individual patient data (IPD) available one trial aggregate-level data (ALD) available another.

title: outstandR: Outcome Regression Standardisation
text: package follows “Strategy” pattern users define adjustment method using strategy_* functions pass main outstandR() wrapper.

title: outstandR: Outcome Regression Standardisation
text: MAIC (Matching-Adjusted Indirect Comparison): Non-parametric likelihood reweighting. G-computation (ML & Bayesian): Model-based standardisation (STC deprecated favour G-computation). MIM (Multiple Imputation Marginalization): Parametric G-computation using multiple imputation.

title: outstandR: Outcome Regression Standardisation
text: outstandR(ipd_trial, ald_trial, strategy, ...): primary user-facing function. strategy_maic(formula, ...): Define MAIC strategy. strategy_gcomp_ml(formula, ...): Define Maximum Likelihood G-computation strategy. strategy_gcomp_bayes(formula, ...): Define Bayesian G-computation strategy. strategy_mim(formula, ...): Define Multiple Imputation Marginalization strategy.

title: Model-Based Standardisation for Indirect Treatment Comparison with Limited Subject-Level Data
text: Population adjustment methods increasingly used compare marginal treatment effects cross-trial differences effect modifiers limited patient-level data. outstandR package allows implementation range methods situation including following: Matching-Adjusted Indirect Comparison (MAIC) based weighting, sensitive poor covariate overlap extrapolate beyond observed covariate space. reweights individual patient-level data (IPD) match aggregate characteristics comparator trial, thereby aligning populations. Parametric G-computation maximum likelihood: method fits outcome model IPD using maximum likelihood estimation, uses model predict outcomes comparator population. allows extrapolation beyond observed covariate space requires correct specification outcome model avoid bias. Parametric G-computation Bayesian inference: Similar maximum likelihood version, approach fits outcome model within Bayesian framework. allows coherent propagation uncertainty prior distributions posterior inference, enabling probabilistic sensitivity analysis full uncertainty quantification. Multiple imputation marginalization method based parametric G-computation: Current outcome regression-based alternatives can extrapolate target conditional treatment effect incompatible indirect comparison. adjusting covariates, one must integrate average conditional estimate relevant population recover compatible marginal treatment effect. can easily applied outcome regression generalized linear model Cox model. approach views covariate adjustment regression nuisance model separates estimation evaluation marginal treatment effect interest. method can accommodate Bayesian statistical framework, naturally integrates analysis probabilistic framework. Simulated Treatment Comparison (STC) [DEPRECATED]: previously common, standard STC can produce biased marginal estimates due aggregation bias non-collapsibility using non-linear link functions. Note: STC formally deprecated v2.0.0 favour robust G-computation approaches.

N: (G-Comp ML, MIM, Bayesian G-Comp) Number iterations. nu, hats.v, M: (MIM ) Imputation parameters matrices.

code: data(AC_IPD_binY_contX)  # A vs C individual patient-level data data(BC_ALD_binY_contX)  # B vs C aggregate-level data  # linear formula lin_form <- as.formula("y ~ PF_cont_1 + PF_cont_2 + trt*EM_cont_1 + trt*EM_cont_2")                                  # sampling values of additional arguments picked for speed # select appropriate to specific analysis  # matching-adjusted indirect comparison outstandR_maic <- outstandR(   AC_IPD_binY_contX, BC_ALD_binY_contX,   strategy = strategy_maic(formula = lin_form, n_boot = 100)) #> Note: Using legacy 'formula' argument. #> --> Analysis Model: y ~ PF_cont_1 + PF_cont_2 + trt * EM_cont_1 + trt * EM_cont_2 #> --> Inferred Balance Model: ~ PF_cont_1 + PF_cont_2 + EM_cont_1 + EM_cont_2 #>     (Balancing on means of these covariates by default) #> Warning: Covariates detected in the MAIC outcome model. To ensure the estimation of compatible marginal treatment effects, MAIC requires an unadjusted outcome model. The outcome model is being automatically overridden to 'y ~ trt'. #>  #> ── Starting outstandR Analysis ───────────────────────────────────────────────── #> ℹ Strategy: maic #>  #> ── MAIC Execution ── #>  #> ℹ Calculating weights using method of moments... #> ℹ Starting Bootstrap with 100 replicates. #> Treatment is guessed as: trt  # simulated treatment comparison outstandR_stc <- outstandR(   AC_IPD_binY_contX, BC_ALD_binY_contX,   strategy = strategy_stc(lin_form)) #> Warning: `strategy_stc()` was deprecated in outstandR 1.X.X. #> ℹ Please use `strategy_gcomp_ml()` instead. #>  #> ── Starting outstandR Analysis ───────────────────────────────────────────────── #> ℹ Strategy: stc  # \donttest{ # G-computation with maximum likelihood outstandR_gcomp_ml <- outstandR(   AC_IPD_binY_contX, BC_ALD_binY_contX,   strategy = strategy_gcomp_ml(lin_form, n_boot = 100, N =100)) #>  #> ── Starting outstandR Analysis ───────────────────────────────────────────────── #> ℹ Strategy: gcomp_ml #>  #> ── G-Computation (ML) Execution ── #>  #> ℹ Fitting initial model... #> ℹ Starting Bootstrap: 100 replicates. #> ℹ Simulating pseudo-pop (N=100) per replicate.  # G-computation with Bayesian inference outstandR_gcomp_bayes <- outstandR(   AC_IPD_binY_contX, BC_ALD_binY_contX,   strategy = strategy_gcomp_bayes(lin_form),   chains = 1, iter = 1000, warmup = 20) #>  #> ── Starting outstandR Analysis ───────────────────────────────────────────────── #> ℹ Strategy: gcomp_bayes #>  #> ── G-Computation (Bayesian) Execution ── #>  #> ℹ Compiling/Sampling Stan model... #>  #> SAMPLING FOR MODEL 'continuous' NOW (CHAIN 1). #> Chain 1:  #> Chain 1: Gradient evaluation took 4.3e-05 seconds #> Chain 1: 1000 transitions using 10 leapfrog steps per transition would take 0.43 seconds. #> Chain 1: Adjust your expectations accordingly! #> Chain 1:  #> Chain 1:  #> Chain 1: WARNING: There aren't enough warmup iterations to fit the #> Chain 1:          three stages of adaptation as currently configured. #> Chain 1:          Reducing each adaptation stage to 15%/75%/10% of #> Chain 1:          the given number of warmup iterations: #> Chain 1:            init_buffer = 3 #> Chain 1:            adapt_window = 15 #> Chain 1:            term_buffer = 2 #> Chain 1:  #> Chain 1: Iteration:   1 / 1000 [  0%]  (Warmup) #> Chain 1: Iteration:  21 / 1000 [  2%]  (Sampling) #> Chain 1: Iteration: 520 / 1000 [ 52%]  (Sampling) #> Chain 1: Iteration: 1000 / 1000 [100%]  (Sampling) #> Chain 1:  #> Chain 1:  Elapsed Time: 0.002 seconds (Warm-up) #> Chain 1:                0.103 seconds (Sampling) #> Chain 1:                0.105 seconds (Total) #> Chain 1:   # Multiple imputation marginalization outstandR_mim <- outstandR(   AC_IPD_binY_contX, BC_ALD_binY_contX,   strategy = strategy_mim(lin_form,                           N = 100), # size of pseudo-population   chains = 1, iter = 1000, warmup = 20) #>  #> ── Starting outstandR Analysis ───────────────────────────────────────────────── #> ℹ Strategy: mim #>  #> SAMPLING FOR MODEL 'continuous' NOW (CHAIN 1). #> Chain 1:  #> Chain 1: Gradient evaluation took 1.4e-05 seconds #> Chain 1: 1000 transitions using 10 leapfrog steps per transition would take 0.14 seconds. #> Chain 1: Adjust your expectations accordingly! title: Simulate Aggregate-Level Data Pseudo-Population — simulate_ALD_pseudo_pop
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{  ## Example 1: Simulating data with explicitly defined marginals and ## a provided correlation matrix (rho)  my_formula <- y ~ x1 + x2*trt + trt  # Define marginal distributions (Normal for age, Lognormal for BMI) dists <- c(x1 = "norm", x2 = "norm")  # Define parameters matching the chosen distributions params <- list(   x1 = list(mean = 60, sd = 8),   x2 = list(mean = 3, sd = 0.1) )  # Define a 2x2 correlation matrix corr_mat <- matrix(c(1, 0.25,                      0.25, 1), nrow = 2,                    dimnames = list(c("x1", "x2"),                                    c("x1", "x2")))  # Generate the synthetic cohort sim_cohort_marginals_rho <- simulate_ALD_pseudo_pop(   formula = my_formula,   ald = mock_ald,   trt_var = "trt",   rho = corr_mat,   N = 100,   marginal_distns = dists,   marginal_params = params )  head(sim_cohort_marginals_rho)  ## Example 2: Estimating the correlation matrix (rho) from provided IPD  # Create some mock Individual Patient Data (IPD) mock_ipd <- data.frame(   x1 = rnorm(200, 60, 8),   x2 = rlnorm(200, 3.3, 0.1) )  mock_ald <- data.frame(   variable = c("x1", "x1", "x2", "x2"),   statistic = c("mean", "sd", "mean", "sd"),   value = c(1000, 500, 0.7, 0.1),   trt = NA )  # Generate the synthetic cohort using IPD for the correlation structure sim_cohort <- simulate_ALD_pseudo_pop(   formula = my_formula,   ipd = mock_ipd,   ald = mock_ald,   trt_var = "trt",   rho = NA,   N = 100 )  head(sim_cohort)  # Generate the synthetic cohort using IPD for the correlation structure # with marginals sim_cohort_marginals <- simulate_ALD_pseudo_pop(   formula = my_formula,   ipd = mock_ipd,   ald = mock_ald,   trt_var = "trt",   N = 100,   marginal_distns = dists,   marginal_params = params )  head(sim_cohort_marginals) } # }

text: MAIC form non-parametric likelihood reweighting method allows propensity score logistic regression model estimated without IPD AC population. mean outcomes \(\mu_{t(AC)}\) treatment \(t = ,B\) AC target population estimated taking weighted average outcomes \(Y\) \(N\) individuals arm \(t\) AB population. Used compare marginal treatment effects cross-trial differences effect modifiers limited patient-level data. $$ \hat{Y}_{} = \frac{\sum_{=1}^{N} Y_{(AB)} w_{}}{\sum_{=1}^{N} w_{}} $$ weight \(w_{}\) assigned \(\)-th individual receiving treatment \(t\) equal odds enrolled AC trial vs AB trial.

title: New strategy objects — strategy_maic
text: G-computation marginalizes conditional estimates separating regression modelling estimation marginal treatment effect versus C. example, regression model observed outcome \(y\) covariates \(x\) treatment \(z\) fitted AC IPD: $$ g(\mu_n) = \beta_0 + \boldsymbol{x}_n \boldsymbol{\beta_1} + (\beta_z + \boldsymbol{x_n^{EM}} \boldsymbol{\beta_2}) \mbox{}(z_n=1) $$ context G-computation, regression model called “Q-model". fitted Q-model, regression coefficients treated nuisance parameters. parameters applied simulated covariates \(x*\) predict hypothetical outcomes subject possible treatments. Namely, pair predicted outcomes, also called potential outcomes, C, generated subject. plugging treatment C regression fit every simulated observation, predict marginal outcome mean hypothetical scenario units treatment C: $$ \hat{\mu}_0 = \int_{x^*} g^{-1} (\hat{\beta}_0 + x^* \hat{\beta}_1 ) p(x^*) dx^* $$ estimate marginal population-average treatment effect versus C linear predictor scale, one back-transforms scale average predictions, taken subjects natural outcome scale, calculates difference average linear predictions: $$ \hat{\Delta}^{(2)}_{10} = g(\hat{\mu}_1) - g(\hat{\mu}_0) $$

title: New strategy objects — strategy_maic
text: difference Bayesian G-computation maximum-likelihood counterpart estimated distribution predicted outcomes. Bayesian approach also marginalizes, integrates standardizes joint posterior distribution conditional nuisance parameters outcome regression, well joint covariate distribution. Draw vector size \(N*\) predicted outcomes \(y*z\) set intervention \(z* \\{0, 1\}\) posterior predictive distribution specific treatment. defined \(p(y*_{z*} | \mathcal{D}_{AC}) = \int_{\beta} p(y*_{z*} | \beta) p(\beta | \mathcal{D}_{AC}) d\beta\) \(p(\beta | \mathcal{D}_{AC})\) posterior distribution outcome regression coefficients \(\beta\), encode predictor-outcome relationships observed AC trial IPD. given : $$ p(y*_{z*} \mid \mathcal{D}_{AC}) = \int_{x*} p(y* \mid z*, x*, \mathcal{D}_{AC}) p(x* \mid \mathcal{D}_{AC}) dx* $$ $$ = \int_{x*} \int_{\beta} p(y* \mid z*, x*, \beta) p(x* \mid \beta) p(\beta \mid \mathcal{D}_{AC}) d\beta dx* $$ practice, integrals can approximated numerically, using full Bayesian estimation via Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling.

title: outstandR 2.0.0
text: Explicit Model Specifications: package architecture fundamentally updated explicitly separate outcome prediction population weighting. formula argument across strategy_*() functions now accepts list containing outcome_model balance_model. conceptual split makes statistical intent methods like MAIC much clearer (.e., defining exactly covariates balance vs. regress ) future-proofs package upcoming doubly-robust methods require models simultaneously. support new architecture, strategy_maic() introduces intelligent defaults missing formula components: traditional single formula passed (legacy support), automatically infers balance_model stripping response treatment variables. balance_model omitted list, acts delayed function defaulting linear sum covariates found aggregate-level data (ALD). outcome_model omitted, gracefully defaults y ~ trt. outstandR() gains new verbose argument (defaulting TRUE), allowing users silence console output simulations repetitive tasks. also introduces proactive warnings computationally expensive operations (e.g., high n_boot MAIC, large N×RN \times R ML G-computation) prevent users thinking session hung. MAIC weight estimation can now balance covariate variances covariances, reducing residual confounding mismatched distributions. Added moments argument strategy definition functions calc_maic(). Setting moments = 2 automatically expands balancing formula include squared terms. Added int argument strategy definition functions calc_maic(). Setting int = TRUE automatically expands balancing formula include two-way interactions covariates. Automatic variance target calculation moments = 2, maic.boot() function now automatically calculates required aggregate target squared terms (E[X2]E[X^2]) internally using base variable’s mean sd provided ALD.
code: # New recommended approach  strategy_maic(   formula = list(     outcome_model = y ~ trt,      balance_model = ~ age + sex   ) )  # Legacy approach (still supported)  strategy_maic(formula = y ~ trt + age + sex)

title: outstandR 2.0.0
text: Fixed critical bug strategy_mim() strategy_gcomp_bayes() covariate uncertainty fully propagated due incorrect looping predicted samples. Significantly improved performance MAIC method refactoring maic.boot() pre-calculate balance outcome matrices, reducing redundant computations bootstrap iterations. guess_treatment_name() (used hood strategies trt_var NULL) now smarter. correctly identifies treatment variable checking duplicated variables interaction terms, falling back last main effect term. check_balance_formula() added provide strict validation balance models. ensures formula one-sided (response variable) throws informative warning treatment variable mistakenly included. check_formula() now explicitly intercepts Surv objects outcome models. throws clear, informative error stating survival data support officially scheduled later version. Improved reproducibility side-effect management switching withr::local_seed() local random seed handling. Updated print.outstandR() method handle delayed balance model functions, providing clearer output auto-generated models. Standardized strategy naming: strategy_gcomp_stan() now strategy_gcomp_bayes() accurately reflect underlying statistical approach.

title: outstandR 2.0.0
text: strategy_stc() now deprecated removed future release. Simulated Treatment Comparison (STC) phased favor G-computation approaches (e.g., strategy_gcomp_ml()), offer better statistical properties population adjustment.

Treatment labels covariate names longer hard-coded. outstandR now dynamically extracts treatment arms (e.g., rather “”, “B”, “C”) general covariates directly input data (5119596). Covariate generation functions separated moved standalone package, simcovariates.

title: outstandR 1.0.0
text: family argument outstandR() expanded support binary (binomial), continuous (gaussian), count data (poisson). Input data can now contain combination binary continuous covariates, specifically upgraded MAIC (4fb1e21). simulate_ALD_pseudo_pop() now accepts user-provided marginal arguments target distribution. can defined optionally via marginal_distns marginal_params strategy_gcomp_ml() strategy_gcomp_bayes(). Users can now explicitly select outcome scale (e.g., log-odds, risk difference, relative risk) using new scale argument outstandR(). Added support optional correlation structures ALD simulations (d48d0ab).

title: outstandR 1.0.0
text: main outstandR() function now returns absolute values addition treatment contrasts (2f1fbd7). Added dedicated print() method outstandR objects cleanly display analysis results (983cb2f). Comprehensive, separate vignettes demonstrating workflows binary, continuous, count outcome data (fc10a68). complete package documentation website built using pkgdown now available via GitHub Pages (4df16f2).
